reports.topic: Household internet access in four countries (2000–2020)

paragraphs.introduction: The line graph illustrates the proportion of households that had internet access in four countries — Germany, Brazil, Japan, and Nigeria — over a twenty-year period from 2000 to 2020.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, internet penetration rose in all four nations over the period, with Germany and Japan reaching near-universal coverage by 2020. Nigeria, however, recorded by far the lowest levels throughout, with a modest upturn only visible in the final decade.
paragraphs.body1: In 2000, Germany and Japan already had relatively high levels of internet access, at around 30% and 25% respectively. Both countries experienced steep and consistent growth over the following two decades, with Germany reaching approximately 93% and Japan 91% by 2020. Brazil began the period at a considerably lower level of roughly 5%, but climbed steadily to around 70% by the end of the period — a fourteenfold increase that represented the most dramatic proportional rise among the four nations.
paragraphs.body2: Nigeria showed a markedly different trajectory. Access remained negligible — below 5% — throughout the 2000s, before rising gradually to around 27% by 2020. While this represented a significant improvement in relative terms, Nigeria's coverage remained substantially lower than that of the other three countries at every point in the period, highlighting a persistent digital divide between the African nation and its counterparts in Europe, South America, and East Asia.

reports.topic: UK visits abroad and popular destinations (1979–1999)

paragraphs.introduction: The line graph illustrates the number of UK residents going abroad from the UK and overseas residents visiting the UK between 1979 and 1999. The bar chart gives information about how many UK residents travelled to five of the most popular countries in 1999. Units are measured in millions of people.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, there was an increase in the number of visits to and from the UK and there were more visits abroad by UK residents than visits to the UK by overseas residents over the period given. Furthermore, the most popular country to visit by UK residents was France in 1999.
paragraphs.body1: According to the line graph, the number of UK residents visiting overseas began at just under 15 million in 1979 and rose significantly to reach over 50 million in 1999. Around 10 million overseas residents visited the UK in the first year before climbing steadily to just under 30 million by the final year.
paragraphs.body2: Regarding the bar chart, in 1999, the two most popular countries were France and Spain (visited by 10 million UK residents and approximately 9 million respectively). The USA and Greece had about 4 million and 3 million visitors. The least popular country visited was Turkey, which had only about 2 million UK residents going there.

reports.topic: Energy production sources in France (1995 and 2005)

paragraphs.introduction: The two pie charts illustrate the proportion of five sources of energy production (coal, gas, nuclear, petrol and other sources) in France in two years (1995 and 2005).
paragraphs.overview: Overall, in both years coal and gas accounted for over half of all energy production, while the least was other energy sources. There was only a very minimal increase in production from gas and coal, whereas nuclear and other sources almost doubled. Petrol was the only energy source to decrease over the period.
paragraphs.body1: Energy produced by coal totaled 29.80% in 1995 and by 2005, it had increased by about 1% to 30.9%. Likewise, the amount of energy generated by gas likewise went up by approximately 1% from 29.63% in the first year to 30.1% by the final year. The use of nuclear power rose significantly from 6.40% in 1995 to 10.10% in 2005. Other sources of energy production accounted for 4.90% but then climbed to 9.10%.
paragraphs.body2: Petrol, on the other hand, produced 29.27% of all energy in 1995 but 10 years later only 19.55% of energy came from this source.

reports.topic: Population age profiles of Yemen and Italy (2000 and 2050)

paragraphs.introduction: The pie charts compare the proportions of Italian and Yemeni citizens in three age groups in 2000 and projections for 2050.
paragraphs.overview: It is clear that Yemen had the younger population in the year 2000, and the same is predicted for the year 2050. The populations of both countries are predicted to get older over the 50 year period.
paragraphs.body1: In 2000 just over half the Yemeni population were under 14, compared to just over 14% of Italians in the same age group. Only a very small percentage of people in Yemen were over 60 at 3.6%, in contrast to nearly a quarter of the Italian population. The largest group for Italy was the 15-59-year-olds with just over 60% while Yemen had 46.3% of its population in this category.
paragraphs.body2: Yemen's average age is set to increase with the proportion of over 60s increasing by just over 2% and the middle group rising by 11%, leaving the youngest group with a decrease of nearly 13%. It is envisaged that by 2050 the number of people in Italy over 60 will jump to 42.3%, the percentage of those in the youngest bracket will drop to 11.5% and the share in the middle category will decrease to 46.2%.

reports.topic: Urban residency in four Asian countries (1970–2040)

paragraphs.introduction: The line chart shows how urban residency has changed in four Asian countries from 1970 to 2040.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, it is clear that urban living will increase in all countries, especially in Malaysia and the Philippines. By 2040, Malaysia will have the highest percentage of people living in cities.
paragraphs.body1: In 1970, the Philippines and Malaysia had similar urban residency rates, around 31 and 30 percent respectively. Thailand and Indonesia had lower rates, with Thailand at 19 percent and Indonesia at 12 percent. Over the next 20 years, Malaysia's rate fluctuated, then rose quickly after 1990. It is projected to exceed 80 percent by 2040. In contrast, the Philippines declined slightly after 1980 but is expected to recover to over 50 percent.
paragraphs.body2: Indonesia has experienced steady growth, surpassing Thailand in 2000 and the Philippines in 2010. It is forecasted to reach 55 percent. Thailand increased gradually and is expected to hit 40 percent by 2040.

reports.topic: Consumer spending in the UK and France (2010)

paragraphs.introduction: The bar chart provides information on five different areas of expenditure in the UK and France in 2010.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, the UK spent more on cars, books, and cameras, while France spent more on computers and perfume, with camera spending showing the most dramatic difference between the two countries.
paragraphs.body1: Specifically, the UK spent over 450,000 pounds on cars, while France spent 55,000 pounds less. The UK also outspent France on books and cameras. Camera spending was the most contrasting, with the UK spending more than twice as much as France.
paragraphs.body2: However, France spent more on computers and perfume. Computer expenditure was about 380,000 pounds in France compared to 350,000 pounds in the UK. For perfume, France spent around 200,000 pounds, while the UK spent slightly less than 150,000 pounds.

reports.topic: Fruit and vegetable consumption in the UK (2001–2008)

paragraphs.introduction: The bar chart illustrates the proportion of women, men and children consuming five servings of fruit and vegetables each day in 8 years from 2001 to 2008 in the UK.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, it can be seen that the highest percentage of all people consuming fruit and vegetables was women, while children consistently accounted for the lowest proportion. Furthermore, a significant increase in the proportion of people eating these food items can be seen over the period given.
paragraphs.body1: The percentage of women who ate fruit and vegetables started at just over 20% in 2001. This figure then rose steadily to reach a peak of almost 35% in 2006, before falling slightly to 30% in the final year.
paragraphs.body2: In terms of men and children eating fruit and vegetables, the rate remained relatively stable in the first three years (about 17% and 12% respectively). From this point, the proportion of men climbed gradually to peak at about 27% in both 2006 and 2007, after which it dropped to about 26%. A similar growth in the figure for children was evident reaching 25% in 2007 and around 24% in the final year.

reports.topic: Student demographics across six university departments (2011)

paragraphs.introduction: The table displays student data across six majors at an Australian university in 2011.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, female students dominated social science subjects, while IT and engineering attracted more male students and international learners.
paragraphs.body1: Humanities and education had the highest female enrolment, at 72 and 68 percent. In contrast, only about 15 percent of females studied IT and engineering. Non-native English speakers preferred technical fields. Half of these students enrolled in IT, followed by science and engineering. Physics attracted 38 percent of this group, while humanities and education saw about 15 percent.
paragraphs.body2: Similarly, international students mostly chose physics and IT, both at 56 percent. Engineering ranked next at 48 percent, while other departments had lower international representation.

paragraphs.introduction: The flow chart depicts the various stages involved in the recycling of waste paper to produce new paper products.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, the process is a complex sequence consisting of six main stages, beginning with the collection of used paper and ending with the creation of usable recycled paper.
paragraphs.body1: To begin the process, waste paper is collected from various sources and transported to a recycling facility. Once it arrives, the paper is sorted by hand to remove any non-paper materials, such as plastic or metal. Following this initial sorting, the paper is placed into a large vat where it is mixed with water and chemicals to create a pulp. This mixture is then heated and agitated to break down the fibers further.
paragraphs.body2: In the subsequent stage, the pulp undergoes a cleaning process to remove inks and glues. This is achieved through a combination of filtering and chemical treatments. After the pulp has been purified, it is pumped onto a moving screen where the water is drained away. The remaining fibers are then pressed between heavy rollers to remove excess moisture and flatten them into sheets. Finally, these sheets are dried and wound onto large rolls, ready to be used for the production of new paper items.

paragraphs.introduction: The diagram illustrates the linear process involved in producing cement, from the initial extraction of raw materials through to the finished product ready for distribution.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, the manufacturing process is linear rather than cyclical and involves six distinct stages. The most significant transformation occurs in the middle of the process, where raw materials are subjected to extreme heat in a rotating kiln before being refined into the final product.
paragraphs.body1: Initially, limestone and clay are extracted from a quarry and fed separately into a crusher. Once crushed, both materials are mixed together and the resulting powder is passed through a rotating mixer to ensure an even blend. Following this, the mixed powder is transferred to a rotating kiln, where it is heated to approximately 1,450 degrees Celsius. This extreme heat transforms the raw mixture into a substance known as clinker.
paragraphs.body2: After the kiln, the clinker is allowed to cool before being ground into a fine powder by a large grinding machine. At this stage, gypsum is added to the ground clinker and the two materials are blended together. The resulting mixture is the finished cement. Finally, the cement is packaged into bags and distributed to construction sites and retailers.

reports.topic: Silkworm life cycle and silk cloth production

paragraphs.introduction: The graphic illustrates the life process of the silkworm and the phases in the manufacture of silk material.
paragraphs.overview: Overall, the lifecycle is a natural process and contains four cyclical phases, beginning with the laying of eggs and ending with the birth of a new moth from a cocoon. The production of silk is, in contrast to the previous stage, a man-made linear process comprising of 6 main stages.
paragraphs.body1: To begin with, eggs are produced by the moth and it takes 10 days for the eggs to hatch into silkworm larvae that feed on mulberry leaves. This stage takes between 4 and 6 weeks before the worms construct a cocoon from silk thread, taking approximately one week. They stay there for around a fortnight, subsequently emerging as moths and the process can begin again.
paragraphs.body2: The first stage in the manufacture of silk is the selection of cocoons and these are then boiled. Once boiled, the silk thread is unwound to a length of between 300 and 900 meters. Finally, the silk can be twisted and weaved into cloth before being dyed.

paragraphs.introduction: The diagram illustrates the process used by the Australian Bureau of Meteorology to forecast weather.
paragraphs.overview: The process includes four stages: collecting information, analyzing data, preparing the forecast, and broadcasting it.
paragraphs.body1: Weather data is gathered using satellites, radar, or buoys. Satellite data appears as images, while radar information is shown on screens or synoptic charts. Buoys also transmit data, displayed as synoptic charts.
paragraphs.body2: After collection, the forecast is created using computers and shared through TV, radio, or automated telephone systems.
